title: How Reading DNA Changed Diagnosis, Screening, and Treatment

lesson.learn:
Genetic testing lets doctors look directly at DNA to find changes that may explain disease, predict risk, or guide treatment. That has transformed medicine because it moves diagnosis from guesswork toward evidence. Instead of waiting for symptoms alone, clinicians can sometimes identify inherited conditions earlier and act sooner.

The biggest change is not just speed. It is precision. Once a mutation, biomarker, or risk profile is known, treatment can be adjusted to fit the patient more closely. That has made genetic testing useful in cancer care, rare disease diagnosis, family planning, and preventive medicine.
lesson.simpler: Genetic testing reads DNA to see what might be causing a disease or how a person might respond to treatment. It helps doctors make smarter choices earlier.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Genetic testing changed medicine by making diagnosis and treatment more specific to the person.
lesson.deeperDive:
Modern genetic testing includes targeted panels, exome sequencing, and full genome sequencing. Each has a different tradeoff between cost, speed, and coverage. In the clinic, these tests can diagnose rare disorders, identify cancer mutations, guide drug dosing, or reveal carrier status for inherited disease.

The challenge is interpretation. A sequence result is only useful if clinicians know what it means, and many variants are still uncertain. That is why genetic counseling and clinical context matter so much. The technology is powerful, but the value comes from combining the data with careful medical judgment.
lesson.funFact: Many genetic tests are most useful not because they give every answer, but because they narrow down the few possibilities that really matter.
references: National Human Genome Research Institute - Genetic testing

people: Francis Collins - Helped bring genomics into medicine
Mary-Claire King - Influential in disease gene discovery
James Watson - Helped launch the DNA era
Kary Mullis - Invented PCR, which supports testing
places: NIH - Major home of human genomics research
Broad Institute - Genomics and disease research center
Mayo Clinic - Clinical genetics and testing center
Genomics laboratories - Where most testing is processed
years: 2010s - Exome and panel testing expand quickly
2020s - Sequencing becomes faster and more accessible
concepts: DNA sequencing - Reading the order of DNA bases
Variant - A difference in DNA sequence
Carrier screening - Testing for inherited risk
Genetic counseling - Help interpreting test results
Biomarker - A measurable sign used in medicine
Precision diagnostics - Testing tailored to the patient
